sources: GolfWRX Forums - Losing Golf Balls Mental Game

quotes.quote: Through many trials, I have really enjoyed honing in on the ball. However, there's a down side. When hitting a bad shot and losing that favorite ball, it kind of adds unneeded activity and stress. There's a cost factor. But, I am more concerned about getting upset about it.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls, the mental game
quotes.context: Mental burden of losing expensive balls

quotes.quote: Golf is such a mental game that if you are worried about losing balls, you aren't playing your best golf.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls, the mental game

quotes.quote: I used to be concerned about losing balls and such...mainly from the financial side of things. Lately, I've been loading up on a ball I love when I find them at great prices...but even before I found those deals, I was seeing a huge difference in my play when I was concerned about losing balls versus playing and swinging freely.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls, the mental game
quotes.context: Financial concern directly impacting swing freedom

quotes.quote: A lost ball is like a dog that bites... Once it bites odds are much higher that it will bite again. I'll take a cursory look but do not spend minutes looking for any ball. There are plenty more to replace it.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls, the mental game
quotes.context: Liberation mindset - not caring about losing balls

quotes.quote: I tee up whatever I grab out of my bag as it is usually a grab bag of ProV1x Practice, Nike RZN Platinum Practice, Srixon XV or Lethal balls. They all perform within a few yards and one spins a little more, another a little less.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls, the mental game
quotes.context: Finding freedom in playing any quality ball

quotes.quote: If I was playing for money I would settle on one ball. Since I pay to play, I use whatever I find a deal on and with the ball deals this year it works out to about $0.70/a ball on average so not something that will entice me to go digging through the weeds, trees, water, etc.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls, the mental game
quotes.context: Smart golfer approach to ball economics

quotes.quote: Price vs performance it's pretty tough to beat the Vice Pro or Pro Plus.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: Snell Prime is a good ball and value, I'd say same for Vice Pro/Pro Plus. LA Golf I feel like with the price they came in at is a reach but its a good ball.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: I'd put any of those next to a prov1/x tp5/x, I haven't had a chance to try this years batch at all yet though and it sounds like Callaway has made some big changes and Bridgestone always has a solid ball imo.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: As far as value (high quality better price) I'd say there is a DTC ball that for most mortals will be as good as what they pay 10 more for.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls
quotes.context: DTC balls comparable for average golfer

quotes.quote: If it's a value play, are any of them better value than when the Srixon Z-Star line-up goes on their half-priced sales several times a year?
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls
quotes.context: Comparing DTC to premium on sale

quotes.quote: The Vice Pro plus and Snell MTB-X are as good as any ball I've ever tried. The yellow/orange fade and lime green pro plus are so visible to track.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: I've tried Snell and Vice and wasn't enamoured with either so if I find any, I give them to a friend.
I've played both balls and think they are both good. Both are a good value compared to premium balls sold retail. I chose Snell because of the founder story.
I've never cared for VICE - too many models that all seem to say the same things. The Snell is great. My college team plays them, I play them.
Very happy with them. I'm sure the premium balls ARE better, but at 4-Times the price they certainly don't make my game 4 times better or 4 times more enjoyable.
Weight consistency falls in the 'Poor' range. Box 1 was a bit heavier than average and Box 3 was quite variable relative to the average.
The diameter consistency of the Performance+ V3 falls within the 'Poor' range. The diameter consistency was off enough that the chart y-axis had to be adjusted accordingly.
quotes.context: Kirkland V3 diameter inconsistency - had to adjust charts

quotes.quote: Due to the significant variability across the sample, compression falls within the 'Poor' range. Compression across the sample ranged from 88 to 103 with a compression delta of 15 points.
quotes.context: Kirkland V3 compression varies by 15 points within same box

quotes.quote: True Price is how we quantify the quality of a golf ball. It's a projection of what you'd have to spend to ensure you get 12 good balls. The greater the difference between the retail price and the True Price, the more you should be concerned about the quality of the ball.
quotes.context: True Price concept - not all balls in dozen are 'good'

quotes.quote: While this may be a value-based urethane ball, if you're seeking the utmost in performance, you may want to look elsewhere. On the other hand, if you're just out to have some fun and place a friendly wager with your foursome, the price is hard to beat.
quotes.context: Kirkland - OK for casual, not for performance seekers

quotes.quote: The True Price for the Kirkland Performance+ V3 is $19.69. That's a 13-percent increase over the retail price of $17.50 per dozen.
quotes.context: Kirkland true cost 13% higher than sticker

quotes.quote: At the time of testing, the Kirkland Performance+ V3 received a Ball Lab score of 45. That's 29 points below the current database average and 43 points below its predecessor, the Costco Kirkland Performance + V2.
quotes.context: Kirkland V3 scored 43 points worse than V2

quotes.quote: While there are not a massive number of bad balls, there is just not much continuity from ball to ball. It is almost as if no two balls are the same and thereby potentially affecting your performance on the course.
I've been playing the V3 balls for a while and they are fine for me. As a high handicapper my swing is wildly more inconsistent than the slight differences in balls that MGS finds.
I happen to have 6 boxes of V3 balls and weighed all of them. Every one weighed between 45.6 and 45.8 grams inclusive. Maybe they got a bad batch but I think a retest is in order.
I tried V2 and they were really spinny. Great around the greens but sucked distance. I stopped playing them though because of wear issues. They scuffed up after just a few holes.
quotes.context: Kirkland V2 scuffing after few holes

quotes.quote: I use kirkland golf balls. I believe them to be just fine for my swing speed and game. Won 1st place in league with the kirkland golf ball.
I knew the results were going to be bad. MGS is anti-Costco golf. What a sham.
New version 'v3.5' came out several months ago that is packaged differently than this lab version. No idea about actual difference but allegedly lower spin.
The old Kirkland balls are better than the new v2, I play decent with just about any balls, upper 70s to low 80s, good enough to out do my buddies most of the time.
quotes.context: Original Kirkland better than new versions

quotes.quote: The only 'good ball' Kirkland produced was the first version, the model they had to stop making & selling due to Acushnet Company (Titleist) lawsuit for patent infringement.
quotes.context: Original Kirkland was the only good one

quotes.quote: Titleist pays insane money to tour pros and other endorsements. All factored into their price. Same for the other big golf companies.
Really simply it means that one ball is going to perform differently than the next. They'll fly differently, spin differently, and ultimately travel different distances.
I get that lots of golfers think it doesn't matter or that they're not good enough to tell the difference (whether you observe it or not, the differences are nonetheless real).
quotes.context: Challenging 'I can't tell the difference' belief

quotes.quote: It's a bit like mixing different models into the same dozen or a bit like if I randomly bent that 9-iron you mentioned a degree or two in either direction every time you pulled it out of the bag.
quotes.context: Ball inconsistency = random club loft changes

quotes.quote: Pick a club you hit, say, 150 yards on average. A 15-20 handicap usually has a pretty wide dispersion at 150. For this exercise, lets say as short as 140 and as long as 160. If the inconsistency of the golf balls you play can take off or add another 5 yards, it could increase your dispersion by another 50%.
quotes.context: Ball inconsistency impact on dispersion - 50% increase

quotes.quote: I have the yellow ones and they seem to scuff up badly after only a few hits. I feel my swing is pretty consistent and every ball I hit seems to fly differently, almost like they are not balanced well.
If you look at the packaging the yellow balls are made in a different plant from a different country. China if I recall correctly. That stopped me from trying the yellow version.
Sounds like the V3 isn't worth it. I love the TM Tour Response. Ever since MGS published a very high score for those the price has sky rocketed. Oh well, you get what you pay for… usually.
In the end. You usually get what you pay for.

I use the Kirkland v3 for hitting into my practice net at home. They're very poor quality golf balls. Cover durability is awful. They breakdown after a few wedge shots. Wouldn't recommend gaming these.
The yellow balls perform MUCH different than the whites. The yellows have noticeably less spin. I wish this site would review the yellow balls, they are the best kept secret in the game.
A 15 Delta difference in compression is huge to me, given that I have a moderate swing speed. One ball will feel like a rock & next will feel much softer. I don't want that inconsistency.

Compression can be surprisingly difficult to nail consistently. It requires precise mixing of the ingredients, and if the raw materials aren't stored properly (climate controlled environment) things can go to hell pretty quickly. Some lower-tier factories have been known to store their raw materials outdoors.
quotes.context: Why value balls have consistency issues

quotes.quote: V3 suits my game. I'm a high handicapper usually 2-3 strokes on the greens. Distance is not a factor. Consistent yardage is. V3 is a short game ball with great spin. Won't change.
It has been estimated that the average golfer loses three to four balls a round, accounting for more than 400 million lost balls globally each year.
quotes.source: Golf.com - 5 ways to stop losing golf balls
quotes.context: Scale of ball loss problem - 400 million/year

quotes.quote: Losing a golf ball on the course can be difficult to swallow, for a number of reasons, but it happens to us all from time to time. The best golf balls on the market are a worthwhile but often costly investment.
quotes.source: GolfMonthly - Worst ways to lose your golf ball

quotes.quote: It's not just the monetary implications that frustrates golfers when they are forced to reluctantly abandon their stranded companion in the weeds, the water or past the dreaded white stakes.
quotes.source: GolfMonthly - Worst ways to lose your golf ball

quotes.quote: Whether you have dumped a brand new Titleist Pro V1 in the water hazard with your first hit or you have tearily said goodbye to the old faithful that helped you break 90 for the first time, I want to hear about it.
quotes.source: GolfMonthly - Worst ways to lose your golf ball

quotes.quote: A dozen Titleist Pro V1 golf balls can set you back $35 a box, so losing that one begins to cut into your golf fun. Losing a ball can not only cost you money, but it can ruin a great day out on the course.
quotes.source: Keiser University - How to find lost golf ball
quotes.context: Financial and emotional impact of ball loss

quotes.quote: Stop searching for the same golf ball Tiger Woods or Nelly Korda plays - it's probably hurting your game more than it's helping.
quotes.source: CaddieHQ - Best golf balls for average golfer
quotes.context: Permission to not play tour balls

quotes.quote: For the average golfer, the key isn't finding a ball that can perform miracles for a 120 mph swing, but one that fits your swing and helps manage your common misses.
quotes.source: CaddieHQ - Best golf balls for average golfer

quotes.quote: The truth is, most high handicappers are playing with the wrong balls – ones that actually exaggerate their mistakes rather than helping correct them.
quotes.source: Best5 Products - Best golf balls for high handicappers

quotes.quote: If you're a high handicapper, you know that sinking feeling when your ball veers wildly off course – that slice into the woods or hook into the water that seems to happen more often than not.
quotes.source: Best5 Products - Best golf balls for high handicappers

quotes.quote: You have probably played golf with someone who, after shaking your hand on the 18th green, says something like, 'Well, I managed to play with the same ball for the whole round'?
quotes.source: GolfMonthly - How many balls do amateurs lose

quotes.quote: Sometimes playing 18 holes without having to reach into your bag for another golf ball can feel like quite an accomplishment.
quotes.source: GolfMonthly - How many balls do amateurs lose

quotes.quote: The 2019 version of the ball is $50, or $45 on sale and the newest version is $50-60 bucks, I'll take the $20 cheaper one.
quotes.source: Reddit - Vice and Snell worth the money

quotes.quote: For the last 3 years, I've played Maxfli and Vice, and both are very good.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: Maxfli Tour and Tour X are a little cheaper, especially with the 4/$99 specials they were running the last month.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: Other than a few I've found, really haven't tried DTC Balls. Although I did once buy some Ksigs Gen 1 and Maxfli. Didn't really notice any difference. I'm sure they are good for a lot of people.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: You do know Taylormade bought the plant that made the first Kirkland ball right. That first Kirkland ball was based on a ball that plant made to sell with another name on it in the Asian market.
The chances of a direct to consumer ball being on level with the Taylormade balls is getting harder if impossible to be made as sources are getting thinner.
The first version was discontinued because the raw ingredients purchased for production of Costco's balls eventually ran out and couldn't be replaced or sourced.
So now I can blame my bad swings on a consumer friendly golf ball. Only in America.
I could never justify playing them. I could easily lose 3 or 4 balls a round at my old course, so the cost, and my lack of talent combined to make them way too expensive.
quotes.source: GolfMonthly Forum - Pro V1 Worth the money?
quotes.context: Ball loss anxiety preventing premium purchase

quotes.quote: I have 6 boxes of V3 balls and weighed all of them. Every one weighed between 45.6 and 45.8 grams inclusive. Maybe they got a bad batch.
Why isn't there a standard compression test for all manufacturers golf balls. It seems a con there isn't.
quotes.context: No industry standard for ball specs

quotes.quote: All of that said … it's less than $20 and at that price point, it's a lot easier to do worse than better.
quotes.context: Value balls still worth it at price point

quotes.quote: Good info, I really liked the 4 piece Kirkland and this article confirmed my suspicions that the new ball is inferior and feels hard as a rock to me.
Too bad. I was hoping they'd be better than last years version. Didn't expect them to be that bad. Hate the variance between balls.

That's disappointing. I would have thought concentricity to be harder to hold than size and compression. If my game was only this inconsistent I would be ecstatic, but for my ball I guess I expect a bit better.
I do not think I will be wasting my money on them anymore.
It is almost as if Costco bought the leftover product from an OEM production run, it had nothing to do with raw materials, COVID, etc.

There are LA, Snell, Vice, etc. I've used Snell but never loved or hated them. I've seen lots of positive reviews for Vice. What are people's thoughts, and is there a brand they think is genuinely high quality for a better price?
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: If it's a performance or fitting play, I can see some benefit to that in terms of it being an additional option for fine-tuning.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Views on DTC golf balls

quotes.quote: It's making me think to use a lower cost ball or ones that I just don't care about.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls mental game
quotes.context: Switching to cheaper balls due to anxiety

quotes.quote: I guess the ball hoarders don't care as they have plenty of ammo.
quotes.source: GolfWRX - Losing golf balls mental game

quotes.quote: We have all been there before. You buy a brand-new box of golf balls and with that first tee shot send a sparkling white ball into the rough. You spend the next 15 minutes searching for it to no avail.
quotes.source: Keiser University - How to find lost golf ball
